titleInfo.title: Seeing through Images: Reconstructing Buddhist Meditative Visualization Practice in Sixth-Century Northeastern China

mods.abstract: About meditative visualizations uniting repentance and meditation used within the Ten Stages lineages / school. Williams holds that"The Buddha Names in Seven Registers" originated within the Ten Stages school; but these origins were lost by the early eighth century. The Three Stages school version of this ritual was an adaptation. Ratnamati (勒那摩提, d. ca. 513, who arrived with Bodhiruci in Luoyang in 508) wrote "Method for Venerating the Buddhas" 禮佛法, his disciple was Huiguang 慧光 (468-537), one of the founders of the Ten Stages lineages. For Huiguang’s meditative practice, Williams points at Lingyu 靈裕 518-605, the most eminent disciple of Daoping (道慿, d. 549), who was in turn the most eminent disciple of Huiguang. Repentance verses by Lingyu and a repentance prayer (十惡懺文) written by Tanqian (曇遷, 542-607)are preserved in Fayuan zhulin 86: 918b10-c22(Chen, Monks and Monarchs, 96 n. 25.) For a summary, see Adamek 2017, 23-25
